past_medical_history: Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 12 years ago, controlled with oral agents (HbA1c 6.9%)
Hypertension, diagnosed 18 years ago, well-controlled on dual therapy
Benign prostatic hyperplasia, diagnosed 5 years ago
Bilateral sensorineural hearing loss, wears hearing aids
family_history: Mother diagnosed with Alzheimer disease at age 76, resided in memory care facility, passed at age 83
Father had hypertension and type 2 diabetes, died of stroke at age 71
Older brother, age 78, recently diagnosed with mild cognitive impairment

social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife of 50 years in a two-story home. Adult daughter lives nearby and visits regularly.
social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, 15 pack-years, quit 20 years ago
social_history.exercise: Previously walked the neighborhood daily and played golf twice a week; has become less motivated for both over the past year

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person and place; knows month and year but states the date is three days off. MMSE 21/30, losing points on recall (0/3 at 5 minutes, no improvement with cues), orientation (2 points lost), serial sevens (3 errors), and pentagons (unable to draw intersecting pentagons accurately). Clock drawing shows numbers clustered on the right side with hour hand placed at the correct number but minute hand pointing to the number 10 rather than the 2 o'clock position. Naming intact for common objects, difficulty naming the band on a watch ('that thing that holds it on'). Repetition intact. Follows two-step commands but fails three-step commands. Spelling WORLD backward: 'DLROW' with self-correction to 'DLRW' (still incorrect).
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. Visual fields full to confrontation. Face symmetric at rest and with activation. Hearing reduced bilaterally (known hearing loss, wearing aids). Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline, no fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk and tone normal throughout. No cogwheel rigidity. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. No pronator drift. No tremor, asterixis, or involuntary movements.
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception in upper extremities. Mildly reduced vibration sense at bilateral great toes (consistent with age and diabetes). Proprioception intact distally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, brachioradialis bilaterally. Patellar reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Achilles reflexes 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No Hoffman sign.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin normal bilaterally. No dysmetria or intention tremor. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady gait with normal base and stride. Mild hesitation on tandem gait but completes without falling. Romberg negative. No festination, no retropulsion.
neurological_exam.additional: No paratonic rigidity. No grasp reflexes. No snout or palmomental reflexes. No myoclonus. Frontal release signs absent.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive memory loss and trouble managing finances over the past two years
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Harold Jenkins is a 74-year-old right-handed retired postal service supervisor who presents to the neurology memory clinic accompanied by his wife, Gloria, and adult daughter, Denise, for evaluation of progressive memory difficulties that have worsened steadily over the past two years. His wife first noticed problems approximately two years ago when Mr. Jenkins began forgetting to pay several household bills, something he had managed independently and reliably for decades. Over the subsequent months, his daughter discovered multiple unopened bills in a drawer and noticed late payment notices accumulating. His wife gradually took over the finances approximately one year ago. He frequently repeats stories and questions within the same conversation, often unaware that he has already told them. His wife reports that he left the back door unlocked several times overnight, which is very uncharacteristic, and on one occasion left the car running in the driveway for over an hour after returning from the barbershop. He has become less engaged in his weekly card game with friends from the postal service, stating that he cannot keep track of the cards as well as he used to. His golf partners have mentioned that he occasionally forgets whose turn it is and has difficulty keeping score accurately. He struggled to program the new television remote control after they recently upgraded their cable service, whereas previously he was comfortable with technology. His wife notes that he occasionally puts items in unusual places, such as placing his wallet in the refrigerator and his reading glasses in the pantry. He continues to dress himself appropriately, manages his own grooming, and recognizes all family members without difficulty. He still drives for short familiar errands but his wife has started accompanying him due to one incident where he took a wrong turn coming home from church, a route he has driven for over thirty years. He has become somewhat more withdrawn socially and occasionally appears frustrated when he cannot find the right word during conversation. He denies any hallucinations, and his family has not observed any personality or behavioral changes apart from mild irritability when he is aware of his mistakes. There have been no episodes of confusion with fluctuating alertness, no tremor, no falls, and no changes in his walking. Sleep is reportedly normal, and his wife has not observed any acting out of dreams or unusual nocturnal behaviors.

signal_characteristics.T1: Prominent bilateral hippocampal sulcal dilatation with widened choroidal fissures, right slightly more pronounced than left
signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: No abnormal signal within hippocampal formations

signal_characteristics.Coronal_T1: Prominent perihippocampal CSF spaces bilaterally with volume loss of entorhinal cortex

signal_characteristics.T1: Widened sylvian fissures and temporoparietal sulci beyond that expected for age

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Confluent periventricular caps and rims with scattered punctate deep white matter hyperintensities

volumetrics.hippocampal_volume: Bilateral hippocampal atrophy, Scheltens MTA score 3 on the right, 2-3 on the left
volumetrics.ventricular_system: Moderate lateral ventriculomegaly, consistent with ex vacuo dilatation from parenchymal volume loss. Third ventricle mildly prominent.
volumetrics.cortical_thickness: Reduced cortical thickness most evident in temporoparietal association cortex bilaterally

additional_observations: No acute infarcts on DWI sequences
No evidence of hemorrhage or microbleeds on SWI
Scattered lacunar infarcts in bilateral basal ganglia, chronic-appearing
Patent major intracranial vessels on MRA
mri.impression: Bilateral hippocampal and medial temporal lobe atrophy (Scheltens MTA grade 2-3 left, grade 3 right) with temporoparietal-predominant cortical volume loss. Moderate periventricular white matter disease (Fazekas grade 2) and scattered chronic lacunar infarcts in bilateral basal ganglia, likely reflecting concurrent small vessel disease in the context of hypertension and diabetes. No acute intracranial pathology. Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Sodium 154 mEq/L (H), Chloride 93 mEq/L (L), BUN 23 mg/dL (H), Glucose 107 mg/dL (H), HbA1c 6.9 % (H).

Neuropsychological_Battery.test: Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Immediate Recall
Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Delayed Recall

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: MMSE 21/30 points (H), MoCA 17/30 points (H), Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Immediate Recall 3rd percentile percentile (H), Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test - Delayed Recall 1st percentile percentile (H), Boston Naming Test 15th percentile percentile (H), Category Fluency (Animals) 8th percentile percentile (H), Phonemic Fluency (FAS) 22nd percentile percentile (H), Trail Making Test Part B 15th percentile percentile (H), Rey Complex Figure - Copy 20th percentile percentile (H), Geriatric Depression Scale 5/15 points (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Trail Making Test Part B: 15th percentile percentile (H)

findings.location: Diffuse cortical uptake, most prominent in prefrontal, precuneus, posterior cingulate, and lateral temporoparietal cortex

signal_characteristics.Distribution: Widespread bilateral cortical amyloid binding with relative sparing of sensorimotor and occipital cortex

additional_observations: Non-specific white matter uptake, slightly higher than typical, may reflect concurrent white matter disease
No focal asymmetry suggesting alternative pathology
output.impression: POSITIVE amyloid PET scan. The elevated SUVr of 1.48 indicates moderate-to-high amyloid burden. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.location: Bilateral temporoparietal cortex, posterior cingulate gyrus, and precuneus

signal_characteristics.FDG_PET: Bilateral reduction in FDG uptake in temporal and parietal association cortices, right slightly more affected than left
signal_characteristics.Pattern: Temporoparietal and posterior cingulate hypometabolism with relative preservation of frontal cortex, primary sensorimotor cortex, occipital cortex, basal ganglia, thalami, and cerebellum

additional_observations: Mild asymmetry with right hemisphere slightly more affected, correlating with slightly greater right hippocampal atrophy on structural MRI
Primary visual cortex metabolism normal, excluding posterior cortical atrophy variant
output.impression: Mild right-greater-than-left asymmetry is noted. No metabolic pattern to suggest frontotemporal dementia, Lewy body dementia, or pure vascular cognitive impairment. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 16 cmH2O. WBC: 2 cells/uL (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 41.8 mg/dL. Glucose: 70.0 mg/dL (ratio 0.58 (serum glucose 118 mg/dL)). Amyloid_beta_42: 352 pg/mL (reference >500 pg/mL) - DECREASED. Amyloid_beta_40: 7800 pg/mL (reference range 5000-12000 pg/mL) - Normal. Amyloid_beta_42_40_ratio: 0.045 (reference >0.065) - DECREASED. Phosphorylated_tau_181: 85 pg/mL (reference <60 pg/mL) - ELEVATED. Total_tau: 520 pg/mL (reference <400 pg/mL) - ELEVATED. 14-3-3_protein: Negative. RT-QuIC: Negative.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 7-8 Hz alpha activity over bilateral occipital regions, below normal range
background.reactivity: PDR attenuates appropriately with eye opening

findings.location: Bilateral temporal regions, right greater than left

artifacts.impact: Mild, did not obscure underlying cerebral activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Not performed due to patient age
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: No photoparoxysmal response; normal photic driving response

output.impression: Mildly abnormal EEG. The posterior dominant rhythm is slowed at 7-8 Hz with intermittent bitemporal theta activity, right greater than left. No epileptiform discharges or electrographic seizures are identified. The right temporal predominance correlates with asymmetric hippocampal atrophy noted on structural MRI. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: Hyperventilation not performed due to patient age. Recording was 30 minutes in awake state only; sleep was not captured.

interactions: Tamsulosin: No direct interaction, but both may cause dizziness; advise caution
No significant interactions with metformin, amlodipine, losartan, or aspirin
warnings: May cause bradycardia; baseline heart rate is 76 bpm, monitor after initiation
GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea, anorexia) are common initially, usually transient
Patient is on aspirin - monitor for GI bleeding as donepezil may increase gastric acid secretion
Avoid concurrent anticholinergic medications which would reduce efficacy
alternatives: Rivastigmine patch 4.6 mg/24hr - alternative cholinesterase inhibitor, may have fewer GI side effects with transdermal formulation
Galantamine 4 mg twice daily - alternative cholinesterase inhibitor

differential.diagnosis: Mixed dementia (Alzheimer disease with cerebrovascular disease)
differential.key_distinguishing: Concurrent vascular risk factors (hypertension, diabetes) and Fazekas grade 2 white matter disease with lacunar infarcts on MRI; however, the amnestic-predominant cognitive profile and hippocampal atrophy pattern favor AD as the primary pathology

Gradual progressive decline rather than stepwise; memory-predominant profile rather than executive-predominant; hippocampal atrophy out of proportion to vascular changes; white matter disease is moderate but insufficient to explain the degree of cognitive impairment
Insidious onset over 2 years; GDS 5/15 indicates only mild mood symptoms; cognitive deficits are severely disproportionate to mood; patient makes effortful attempts on testing rather than giving up
No visual hallucinations, no parkinsonism on exam (no rigidity, no tremor, no bradykinesia), no REM sleep behavior disorder, no fluctuating alertness or cognition
No prominent personality or behavioral changes; memory impairment is the leading symptom rather than disinhibition, apathy, or language deficits; preserved social decorum

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history from patient and at least one reliable informant, including timeline of cognitive decline, specific examples of functional impairment, and behavioral or psychiatric symptoms
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Progressive amnestic cognitive decline over 2 years with functional impairment in financial management and instrumental activities confirmed by wife and daughter

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination including standardized cognitive assessment (MMSE and/or MoCA)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: MMSE 21/30 with prominent recall failure (0/3), visuospatial errors on clock drawing and pentagons; neurological exam otherwise non-focal except mildly reduced vibration at toes

optimal_actions.action: Order dementia screening laboratories: CBC, BMP, TSH, vitamin B12, folate, RPR, HIV
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reversible causes of dementia excluded; mildly elevated glucose and HbA1c consistent with known diabetes

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with volumetric/hippocampal protocol
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bilateral hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens grade 2-3), temporoparietal cortical volume loss, moderate white matter disease and lacunar infarcts reflecting concurrent small vessel disease
Multidomain amnestic dementia profile with severely impaired delayed recall, impaired naming and semantic fluency, consistent with early Alzheimer disease

optimal_actions.action: Consider amyloid PET or CSF biomarkers to confirm Alzheimer pathology, particularly given concurrent vascular disease on MRI
optimal_actions.expected_finding: CSF profile showing decreased Abeta42, elevated phospho-tau and total tau confirming A+T+N+ Alzheimer disease pathology

optimal_actions.action: Initiate cholinesterase inhibitor therapy (donepezil 5 mg daily) after checking drug interactions
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No significant interactions; counseling on GI side effects and monitoring aspirin co-administration for GI bleeding risk

optimal_actions.action: Ensure vascular risk factor optimization: confirm diabetes and hypertension are well controlled
optimal_actions.expected_finding: HbA1c 6.9% (reasonable control), BP 142/82 (may need tighter control)

optimal_actions.action: Provide diagnosis disclosure and counseling to patient and family including discussion of prognosis, safety planning (driving evaluation), advance directives, and community resources such as Alzheimer's Association
Schedule follow-up in 3-6 months to assess medication tolerance, cognitive trajectory, and functional status

critical_actions: Exclude reversible causes of dementia with laboratory testing (TSH, B12, RPR, HIV)
Perform standardized cognitive assessment (MMSE, MoCA, or neuropsychological battery)
Obtain collateral history from reliable informant (spouse, family member)
Assess functional status and safety including driving ability and financial management capacity
Discuss diagnosis, prognosis, and safety planning with patient and family
Optimize vascular risk factors (diabetes, hypertension) given concurrent cerebrovascular disease
contraindicated_actions: Prescribing anticholinergic medications (e.g., diphenhydramine, oxybutynin) which would worsen cognitive function
Prescribing benzodiazepines without careful consideration of worsened cognitive impairment and fall risk
Attributing cognitive symptoms solely to normal aging or depression without formal evaluation
Starting memantine monotherapy in mild disease (not indicated until moderate-to-severe stage)
Ordering brain biopsy (not indicated with this clinical and biomarker profile)
Prescribing antipsychotics without clear behavioral indication (increased mortality risk in dementia patients)
Discontinuing aspirin without cardiovascular risk assessment (patient has multiple vascular risk factors)
key_reasoning_points: Progressive amnestic cognitive decline over 2 years with insidious onset is the classic presentation of early Alzheimer disease
Functional impairment in financial management and instrumental activities (bill-paying, scorekeeping, technology use) distinguishes dementia from mild cognitive impairment
MMSE 21/30 with complete recall failure (0/3 even with cues) and visuospatial errors is characteristic of early AD
Family history of Alzheimer disease in mother and cognitive impairment in brother increases diagnostic confidence
MRI showing bilateral hippocampal atrophy (Scheltens 2-3) with temporoparietal volume loss is the hallmark imaging pattern of AD

The concurrent white matter disease (Fazekas grade 2) and lacunar infarcts raise the question of mixed pathology, but the amnestic-predominant profile and hippocampal atrophy favor AD as the primary process
Normal dementia screening labs (TSH, B12, RPR, HIV) exclude treatable causes of cognitive decline
Absence of parkinsonism, hallucinations, REM sleep behavior disorder, behavioral changes, or fluctuating cognition argues against Lewy body dementia and frontotemporal dementia
African American patients may present later to memory clinics and have higher prevalence of concurrent vascular risk factors, making confirmation of AD pathology with biomarkers particularly valuable

key_educational_points: Early Alzheimer disease classically presents with progressive episodic memory loss as the predominant symptom
Concurrent cerebrovascular disease is common in elderly patients and does not exclude AD as the primary diagnosis
APOE e4 homozygosity (E4/E4) confers the highest genetic risk for late-onset AD
CSF biomarkers (A+T+N+ profile) can confirm AD pathology in vivo, especially useful when concurrent vascular disease complicates the clinical picture
Cholinesterase inhibitors are first-line pharmacotherapy; vascular risk factor optimization is also important in mixed presentations
Functional assessment (finances, driving, cooking) is essential for staging and safety planning
